stem: A solid is bounded below by the paraboloid \( z = [[k]](x^2+y^2) \) and above by the plane \( z = [[topc]] \). Using cylindrical coordinates, find its volume.
solution: The paraboloid \(z=[[k]]r^2\) meets the plane \(z=[[topc]]\) at \(r=[[R]]\). With \(dV = r\,dz\,dr\,d\theta\), the volume is \( \int_{0}^{2\pi}\int_{0}^{[[R]]}\int_{[[k]]r^2}^{[[topc]]} r\,dz\,dr\,d\theta = \int_{0}^{2\pi}\int_{0}^{[[R]]} r([[topc]]-[[k]]r^2)\,dr\,d\theta = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the Jacobian factor \(r\), integrating \(([[topc]]-[[k]]r^2)\) without it.
This uses the volume of the full cylinder of height \([[topc]]\), ignoring the paraboloid bottom.
This keeps only \(\tfrac14\) of the correct value (a mishandled \(r\)-integral).
This adds a stray multiple of \(\pi\) to the correct value.
This subtracts a stray multiple of \(\pi\) from the correct value.

stem: A solid is bounded below by the cone \( z = [[k]]\sqrt{x^2+y^2} \) and above by the plane \( z = [[topc]] \). Using cylindrical coordinates, find its volume.
solution: The cone \(z=[[k]]r\) meets the plane \(z=[[topc]]\) at \(r=[[R]]\). With \(dV = r\,dz\,dr\,d\theta\), the volume is \( \int_{0}^{2\pi}\int_{0}^{[[R]]}\int_{[[k]]r}^{[[topc]]} r\,dz\,dr\,d\theta = \int_{0}^{2\pi}\int_{0}^{[[R]]} r([[topc]]-[[k]]r)\,dr\,d\theta = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the volume of a full ball of radius \([[R]]\), an unrelated formula.
This is the volume of the whole cylinder of height \([[topc]]\), ignoring the cone bottom.
This drops the factor of \(\tfrac13\) that comes from the cone's linear taper.
This adds a stray multiple of \(\pi\) to the correct value.
This subtracts a stray multiple of \(\pi\) from the correct value.

stem: A solid lies between two paraboloids: \( z = [[a]](x^2+y^2) \) from below and \( z = [[h]] - [[b]](x^2+y^2) \) from above (the two surfaces meet where \( x^2+y^2 = [[R]]^2 \)). Using cylindrical coordinates, find the volume of the solid.
solution: With \(dV = r\,dz\,dr\,d\theta\), the volume is \( \int_{0}^{2\pi}\int_{0}^{[[R]]}\int_{[[a]]r^2}^{[[h]]-[[b]]r^2} r\,dz\,dr\,d\theta = \int_{0}^{2\pi}\int_{0}^{[[R]]} r\left([[h]]-([[a]]+[[b]])r^2\right)dr\,d\theta = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This treats the top as a flat cap at height \([[h]]\) over the full disk, ignoring both paraboloids' curvature.
This uses a sphere-like \(\tfrac43\pi\) coefficient, an unrelated formula.
This is exactly half the correct value (a dropped factor of 2 from the \(\theta\)-integral).
This adds a stray multiple of \(\pi\) to the correct value.
This subtracts a stray multiple of \(\pi\) from the correct value.

stem: A cylindrical shell is formed by drilling a coaxial cylindrical hole of radius \([[a]]\) out of a solid cylinder of radius \([[b]]\) and height \([[H]]\). Using cylindrical coordinates, find the volume of the shell that remains.
solution: With \(dV = r\,dz\,dr\,d\theta\), the volume is \( \int_{0}^{2\pi}\int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]}\int_{0}^{[[H]]} r\,dz\,dr\,d\theta = 2\pi [[H]] \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} r\,dr = \pi [[H]] \left([[b]]^2-[[a]]^2\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to drill out the inner hole, using the full solid cylinder's volume.
This subtracts the radii before squaring instead of squaring first, i.e. \((b-a)^2\) instead of \(b^2-a^2\).
This uses a lateral-surface-area style formula instead of a volume formula.
This adds a stray multiple of \(\pi\) to the correct value.
This subtracts a stray multiple of \(\pi\) from the correct value.

stem: A solid cylinder of radius \([[R]]\) and height \([[H]]\) has mass density \( \delta(x,y,z) = [[k]]\sqrt{x^2+y^2} \) (proportional to distance from the central axis). Using cylindrical coordinates, find the total mass of the solid.
solution: In cylindrical coordinates \(\delta = [[k]]r\) and \(dV=r\,dz\,dr\,d\theta\), so the mass is \( \int_{0}^{2\pi}\int_{0}^{[[R]]}\int_{0}^{[[H]]} [[k]]r\cdot r\,dz\,dr\,d\theta = 2\pi [[H]] [[k]] \int_{0}^{[[R]]} r^2\,dr = \dfrac{2\pi [[k]] [[H]] [[R]]^3}{3} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses only one factor of \(r\) (density times \(dr\) instead of density times the Jacobian \(r\,dr\)), i.e. treats mass like a uniform-density volume.
This uses a sphere-like \(\tfrac43\pi\) coefficient, an unrelated formula.
This uses \(r^4\) instead of \(r^3\) in the radial integral, an extra power of \(r\).
This adds a stray multiple of \(\pi\) to the correct value.
This subtracts a stray multiple of \(\pi\) from the correct value.

stem: A wedge is cut from a solid cylinder of radius \([[R]]\) and height \([[H]]\) by two planes through the central axis, taking the sector where \( 0 \le \theta \le \dfrac{2\pi}{[[n]]} \) (i.e. \(\tfrac{1}{[[n]]}\) of a full revolution). Using cylindrical coordinates, find the volume of the wedge.
distractor_rationales: This uses the volume of the full cylinder, forgetting to take only the \(\tfrac1{[[n]]}\)-fraction wedge.
This has an extra stray factor of \(\tfrac12\) from double-counting the wedge fraction.
This is twice the correct value (a doubled wedge angle).
This adds a stray multiple of \(\pi\) to the correct value.
This subtracts a stray multiple of \(\pi\) from the correct value.
